topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien under my fingertips. It was a biography. Every carefully curated detail, every celebrated triumph, every whispered insecurity—it was all there, meticulously rendered, and none of it was mine. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary sound in the quiet study. I stared at the signature, a name that wasn't mine, and a slow, cold burn started in my chest. I didn't crumble. Instead, I folded the document with deliberate, sharp creases, the sound echoing unnervingly in the silence. "This," I muttered, my voice low and steady, "is a fabrication." I stood up, the chair scraping back against the polished floor, and walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights. I wouldn't let them rewrite the narrative.

[story 2]

The revelation hit me like a physical blow—a sudden, dizzying lurch that made the room tilt. I reread the passage describing my 'defining moment,' the words tasting like ash. A tremor ran through my hands, but I clenched them into fists so tight my nails bit into my palms. I didn't scream. Instead, I picked up the pen that had authored this life story, turning it over and over. My jaw tightened until my molars ached. I let out a short, humorless laugh, a sound devoid of warmth. "You think you own the script?" I challenged the empty air, my eyes locked on the blank page where my true story should have been. I walked over to the desk, not to destroy the paper, but to carefully place it back down, a silent, unyielding statement of refusal.

[story 3]

When the solicitor handed over the bound volume, the sheer audacity of it stole the air from my lungs. Every accolade, every personal anecdote, every perceived flaw—it was all someone else’s carefully constructed edifice. A strange, vibrating energy surged beneath my skin, a hot, restless current that demanded release. I didn't cry. Tears felt too weak for the magnitude of the betrayal. I took the book, its weight grounding me, and walked out onto the balcony. The wind whipped my hair across my face, and I let the force of it buffet me. I planted my feet wide on the cold stone railing. "Let them have their polished lies," I called out over the sound of the traffic below, my voice ringing with a brittle, hard certainty. "I know the real version."
